entries.title: delegation
entries.description: Delegate work to Cursor composers instead of spending Claude limits, and never trust what an agent reports about itself. Three modes: research (scouts whose every cited URL is checked against the tool log), implement (one writer on an isolated branch behind acceptance gates and a repair loop), transform (a parallel pool applying one rule across independent files). Acceptance is deterministic and observable-effect based; a partial result counts as a failure, and a run with no oracle is reported as unverified rather than green.

entries.title: rig
entries.description: rig — a quality-gated AI workbench for Claude Code. Give /rig:go a natural-language task: it classifies the task, runs it in an isolated git worktree, and checks the result against a per-task-type acceptance gate — the diff only lands in your working tree on an explicit accept. Machine sensors back the gate deterministically (secret leak, prompt-injection markers, gate tampering, destructive commands, OpenAPI schema drift), with recorded overrides as the escape hatch. Track parallel tasks with board and the read-only cockpit; measure whether the gate actually works with drill (seeded-bug reviewer detection rates) and stats (rubber-stamp detection). Compose recipes, personas, and knowledge as reusable bricks; a deterministic orchestrator (orchestrate.py) runs recipe state machines with parallel verification, cost-tier auto-routing learned from run history, A/B recipe experiments, and token/cost metering. Reaches beyond a live session via a stdlib-only MCP server, a GitHub Action, Slack/Teams notifications, Codex/Cursor native-layer adapters, and a read-only VS Code sidebar. Feedback loops close the gap between accept-time predictions and reality: production-outcome tracing, signed provenance, cross-session instinct learning with decay, and gap prescriptions that draft /rig:forge improvement requests. Japanese writing quality is first-class: an AI-smell catalog with cognitive-rhythm grounding and a deterministic prose-rhythm sensor.

entries.title: growmax-skills
entries.description: Growmax team Claude Code skills and workflows. Auto-discovers skills/, commands/, agents/, and hooks/. Ships: github-repo-analyzer (skill); terse-mode (skill — low-token terse reply style, in-house alternative to caveman-style compressors; trigger with 'terse mode'/'be terse'/'caveman mode', levels lite|full|off); learn-app + product-manager (per-repo product notebook — /learn-app builds and maintains docs/product/ from a code census + optional read-only walk, with an async question ledger the human answers in-file; the product-manager agent answers questions and writes specs from the notebook with per-claim provenance, never inventing product truth); feature-review (end-of-development multi-agent review — TDD compliance, contextual-creation UX, UI-standards compliance (compose-never-fork + tokens, per .claude/UI-STANDARDS.md), 10k-row scalability + security sweep, advisory architecture ideas — one verified scorecard with a per-finding HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW confidence report, a run ledger, and a periodic-calibration process for improving precision over time); ux-audit + ux-migrate (census UI drift against the repo's UI standard into an approvable backlog, then pay it down autonomously by composing shared components — mirrors e2e-map + e2e-batch; a write-time hook nudges the mechanical rules for free); ui-builder (pre-build shared-component resolver — /ui-builder maps a UI request to the exact shared component per .claude/UI-STANDARDS.md Table 1 with a reuse/extend/new verdict BEFORE code is written; ships the ui-builder-resolver agent and a prompt-time reminder hook, gated to repos that ship the rulebook); app-cartograph (map a live app into durable context — navigation + business flows + knowledge graph — via a code-seeded frontier and a write-safe human-in-the-loop walk; runs upstream of E2E); e2e-flow (gated multi-agent E2E test-generation for a single flow, web + api); and e2e-map + e2e-batch (whole-app flow census → one approval → autonomous suite generation).

entries.title: Three Rivals, One Classroom: AFT's AI Coalition
entries.description: OpenAI, Microsoft, and Anthropic compete everywhere else — but co-funded the same $23M union program to train 400,000 teachers. Here's why.

entries.title: What OpenAI's Free Teacher AI Course Covers
entries.description: OpenAI Academy runs two free courses for K-12 teachers: AI Foundations and ChatGPT Foundations for Teachers. Here's the actual syllabus, section by section.

entries.title: Inside the AI Skills Jam for K-12 Educators
entries.description: OpenAI's AI Skills Jam took the teacher training on the road — in-person workshops across US cities that drew 1,600+ educators. Here's what the format looks like.

entries.title: ChatGPT Foundations for Teachers, Reviewed
entries.description: A fair critical review of OpenAI's free teacher course — what it teaches well, and the gaps around AI grading, hallucination, and detection it glosses over.

entries.title: Can a Course Make 400,000 Teachers AI-Literate
entries.description: The AFT's academy promises to train 400,000 teachers on AI in five years. But scale and depth pull against each other — can standardized training build real literacy?

entries.title: The Cheating Numbers Behind ChatGPT for Teachers
entries.description: A Study.com survey found over a quarter of teachers have caught a student cheating with ChatGPT. Here's what that number actually measures — and what it doesn't.

entries.title: Why Detection Tools Miss 1 in 5 AI Essays
entries.description: AI-writing detectors run roughly 80-90% accurate — and most don't publish rates at all. Here's why that's not good enough to be a teacher's primary defense.

entries.title: Baltimore, LA, NYC, Seattle: Why They Banned ChatGPT
entries.description: Major districts banned ChatGPT over integrity and quality fears. Now OpenAI is courting those same districts with free teacher accounts. Are the two positions contradictory?

entries.title: What the MIT Cognitive Engagement Study Found
entries.description: An MIT study found students who used ChatGPT to write essays showed weaker cognitive engagement. Here's what that implies for student use — and why teacher use is a different case.

entries.title: Oregon Teachers Are Calling AI Deals a "Grift"
entries.description: An Oregon NEA rank-and-file caucus wants an end to what it calls 'AI grift.' Here's what that grassroots pushback represents — and what it doesn't, within a mixed union landscape.

entries.title: Free for Teachers, Banned for Students: The Split
entries.description: OpenAI is courting teachers with a free workspace while districts ban ChatGPT for students. Is that a contradiction — or two different problems wearing one name?

entries.title: The Conflict-of-Interest Question in AI Training
entries.description: AI vendors are funding the union that trains teachers to use their tools. That's a real conflict worth naming — and the safeguards deserve a fair hearing too.

entries.title: Claude for Education: The Other Free Offer
entries.description: OpenAI isn't the only company giving teachers AI for free. Anthropic's education play — a free K-12 offer plus a paid, partner-rich tier — is the quieter alternative.

entries.title: Gemini for Education: The Google Gravity Case
entries.description: For a Google-shop district, the AI question isn't which model is smarter. It's whether Workspace gravity and NotebookLM beat a free standalone ChatGPT.

entries.title: ChatGPT for Teachers vs Microsoft Copilot in Class
entries.description: Same GPT-5.1 engine, different chassis. A free standalone workspace versus an Office-native paid tier — and why the model isn't what decides this one.

entries.title: The Real Price of "Free": A Cost Comparison
entries.description: ChatGPT for Teachers is free per seat — but adoption isn't. Here's the true cost of rolling it out versus paid Gemini, Copilot, and Claude for schools.

entries.title: Context Windows, Compared for Classroom Use
entries.description: Token context sounds abstract until you try uploading a whole class set of essays. Here's what 400K vs 1M–2M tokens actually means for a teacher's day.

entries.title: Why OpenAI Chose Free Over a Freemium Tier
entries.description: OpenAI could have shipped a discounted teacher tier. It shipped a fully free one through June 2027. The go-to-market logic behind that choice is worth reading.

entries.title: The Four-Way AI Vendor Race for the Classroom
entries.description: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Microsoft are all racing for the same classrooms — with four different strategies. Here's how the board actually looks.

entries.title: Building a Custom GPT for Your Department
entries.description: A custom GPT turns a good prompt into a reusable tool your whole department shares. Here's how to build a rubric-grader or differentiation GPT that actually gets used.

entries.title: Co-Planning Lessons in Shared Projects
entries.description: How ChatGPT for Teachers' shared projects turn department co-planning from a Friday scramble into a durable, shared workspace. A realistic PLC workflow.

entries.title: Turning File Uploads Into Faster Grading Prep
entries.description: How file uploads in ChatGPT for Teachers speed up rubric-building and feedback drafting — without letting AI grade for you or auto-send anything to students.

entries.title: Writing Parent Emails Without the Dread
entries.description: How ChatGPT for Teachers helps draft difficult parent emails — setting tone, structuring hard news, and translating — with the rule to always personalize and review first.

entries.title: Differentiation and IEP Support, Carefully Done
entries.description: How to use ChatGPT for Teachers to draft differentiated materials and IEP-aligned supports — and exactly where FERPA caution around student-identifying data has to stop you.

entries.title: Image Generation for Worksheets and Slides
entries.description: How to use ChatGPT for Teachers' image generation for worksheets, slides, and classroom visuals — and why every AI-generated diagram needs an accuracy check first.

entries.title: When Voice Mode Beats Typing in the Classroom
entries.description: Voice mode isn't a gimmick for teachers. It wins in the exact moments a classroom leaves your hands full and your keyboard out of reach. Here's when to talk instead of type.

entries.title: A Procurement Checklist for ChatGPT for Teachers
entries.description: It's free, so districts skip procurement. That's the mistake. Here's the checklist an IT director should run before ChatGPT for Teachers reaches a single classroom.

entries.title: SSO, MFA, and the District IT Rollout Plan
entries.description: The mechanics of a managed ChatGPT for Teachers deployment: claim the domain, wire up SAML SSO, enforce MFA, and onboard teachers in phases without flooding the help desk.

entries.title: Reading the Student Data Privacy Agreement
entries.description: OpenAI names itself a FERPA 'School Official' and says student data stays under the school's control. Here's what those terms actually commit — and what a privacy officer should still ask.

entries.title: Role-Based Access Control for School Workspaces
entries.description: Who should be an admin, who should be a member, and the RBAC misconfigurations that quietly hand a whole district's AI workspace to the wrong people. A practical guide for K-12 IT.

entries.title: Piloting ChatGPT for Teachers District-Wide
entries.description: A free tool still costs you a rollout. How to pilot ChatGPT for Teachers across a district — cohort selection, a real feedback loop, and the scale-or-stop decision.

entries.title: What a Free Vertical AI Product Means for EdTech
entries.description: When the model vendor ships a free, polished, education-specific product directly to teachers, the ecosystem built on its general-purpose API has to rethink what it's for.

entries.title: The Squeeze on Third-Party Classroom AI Tools
entries.description: Paid lesson-planning and differentiation tools now compete with something free from the model vendor itself. What the squeeze looks like, and which tools escape it.

entries.title: What Claude Skill Builders Can Learn From OpenAI
entries.description: OpenAI's education playbook — narrow vertical, distribution through a trusted intermediary, free to build the habit — is a GTM lesson for anyone building a Claude Code skill.

entries.title: Where AI Tutoring Skills Fit Between the Giants
entries.description: ChatGPT for Teachers and Claude for Teachers are both teacher-facing productivity tools. The student-facing Socratic tutor is a niche neither giant is actually filling.

entries.title: Building the Skill a Free Chatbot Can't Replace
entries.description: A free, general teacher assistant is genuinely useful — and structurally incapable of three things a purpose-built skill does. Here's the line between them.

entries.title: Choosing a Pilot: A Decision Guide for Districts
entries.description: ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Copilot for your district AI pilot? A practical framework built on the stack you already run, real per-seat prices, and priorities.

entries.title: No, Students Don't Get Free ChatGPT Too
entries.description: ChatGPT for Teachers is a teacher-and-staff offer, not a student one — and several big districts ban student ChatGPT outright. Untangling the confusion.

entries.title: What Happens When the Free Period Ends in 2027
entries.description: ChatGPT for Teachers is free only through June 2027. OpenAI hasn't said what comes next. Here's what's known, what's speculation, and what to plan for regardless.

entries.title: The Questions Every Teacher Asks Before Signing Up
entries.description: Eligibility, verification, cost, data privacy, and what happens if you change schools — the practical FAQ for ChatGPT for Teachers, and a look back at the series.

entries.title: Domain Name to Live DNS in Twenty Minutes
entries.description: Registrar tradeoffs, why to route DNS through Cloudflare, the DNSSEC gotcha that takes domains offline, and the orange-vs-grey cloud rule for GitHub Pages.

entries.title: Ship a Static Site With Nothing but Git Push
entries.description: Why GitHub Pages beats Vercel, Netlify, and a VPS for a solo founder's first landing page: zero cost, no third-party login, and the deploy is a git push.

entries.title: The ITIN Path: A US Tax ID Without a US Address
entries.description: What an ITIN is, why a Certifying Acceptance Agent beats mailing your passport to the IRS, the 8-12 week timeline, and why it's the first US-entity domino.

entries.title: Your First US Bank Card Without Setting Foot in America
entries.description: The four-piece kit a non-resident founder needs before a US bank says yes: ITIN, a CMRA-clean address, a US SIM, and a Wise account, plus the card application.

entries.title: Oracle's Always-Free Tier: The Server Everyone Forgets
entries.description: Oracle Cloud quietly gives away a permanent 4-core, 24GB-RAM ARM server that would cost $80-100/month on AWS. Here's why signups get rejected, the region choice you can't undo, and how to actually get the machine.

entries.title: Keeping Your Free Oracle VPS From Getting Reclaimed
entries.description: Oracle's free tier has one real string attached: it reclaims idle instances. Here's what 'idle' actually means for a dev box, the keep-alive pattern that works, why community scripts are a fragile bet, and the fix that ends the problem for good.

entries.title: Deploy Global Infrastructure for Free on Cloudflare
entries.description: Cloudflare Workers and Pages replace 'rent a VPS and configure nginx' with 'push code, it runs in 335 cities.' Here's why serverless-at-the-edge is a different model, where the free tier ceiling actually sits, and how Pages and Workers are the same primitive.

entries.title: Build a Waitlist Page in 30 Minutes, Zero Servers
entries.description: A concrete 6-step path from empty folder to a live email-capture page — frontend, backend, and database — deployed worldwide on Cloudflare for $0, before a single line of the real product exists. The fastest way to test if anyone wants the thing.

entries.title: Access Your Home Server From Anywhere, No Open Ports
entries.description: Tailscale turns every device you own into one private network — no public IP, no firewall rules, no forwarded ports. Here's the connection model that makes it work, and the one thing that quietly leaves you stuck on relay latency.

entries.title: Give Your AI Agent Legs: Control It From Your Phone
entries.description: The real payoff of a personal mesh VPN: run an AI coding agent on a home machine and drive it from your phone while the task keeps running. Here's the latency math, the security details that matter, and the one hard limit to plan around.

entries.title: Wyoming vs. Delaware vs. California for a Solo LLC
entries.description: Why an LLC beats a C-Corp for most solo founders, and which state to form in. Wyoming's $60-a-year default, Delaware's investor pull, and California's $800 franchise-tax trap — plus the registered-agent requirement nobody explains until you're stuck.

entries.title: Getting an EIN Without a Social Security Number
entries.description: No SSN? You can still get an EIN by phone or fax — the widely-repeated claim that the IRS killed the fax channel is wrong. Here's the SS-4 prep that keeps you from getting rejected: Line 7b, Line 11, Line 15, and the Responsible Party rule.

entries.title: The Compliance Deadline That Costs $25,000 If You Miss It
entries.description: Most 'form a US LLC' guides stop at the EIN. The part they skip can cost $25,000: Form 5472, required annually from foreign-owned LLCs even at zero revenue. Plus the BOI rule that changed in 2025 and the state report you still owe.

entries.title: Search Traffic Forked: SEO and GEO Are Different Games
entries.description: Search just split into two paths: the ranked blue-link list and the AI-generated answer that quotes a few sources nobody clicks. SEO races for rank; GEO races to be the source the AI draws from. Here's why the distinction reorders how solo founders get found.

entries.title: Why AI Engines Cite Reddit Over Your Homepage
entries.description: AI engines trust what others say about you far more than what you say about yourself. Muck Rack found 82% of AI citations come from earned media, under 6% from brand-owned pages. The real work of GEO isn't polishing your copy — it's getting mentioned elsewhere.

entries.title: The Reddit Playbook for Solo Founders Who Hate Marketing
entries.description: Reddit is Perplexity's #1 source at 46.7% of top citations and #1 in Google AI Overviews at 21%. One genuinely useful 30-minute answer keeps getting pulled into AI responses indefinitely. Here's the minimal-effort playbook — and why this window won't stay open.

entries.title: Ten Schema Tags That Get You Cited by AI
entries.description: Schema markup alone does nothing — Ahrefs tested 1,885 pages and adding it with no content change produced no citation lift. But Organization and FAQPage JSON-LD on top of real content earn 2.7x more AI citations. Here's the code and the deployment checklist.

entries.title: Pillar Pages and Content Clusters for AI Citation
entries.description: Digital Applied found 86% of AI citations come from sites with 5+ interlinked pieces on one topic. Yext found a bidirectionally-linked cluster gets cited 2.7x more than an isolated page. AI crawlers read internal links as a map — here's how to structure content as one knowledge node.

entries.title: Why Stripe Won't Let You Take Payments Yet
entries.description: You created a Stripe account, sent a payment link, and hit 'your account cannot currently make live charges.' Here's what actually activates live mode — and the shortest path from zero to a real first dollar.

entries.title: Merchant of Record, Explained: Paddle vs. Stripe
entries.description: Stripe processes payments; Paddle becomes the legal seller. For a solo founder selling digital products across borders, that distinction decides who handles VAT, GST, refunds, and chargebacks — you or them.
